सेवा में, शाखा प्रबंधक महोदय, पंजाब नेशनल बैंक, शाखा फर्रुखाबाद विषय: खाते में ऑनलाइन ट्रांजैक्शन पुनः चालू कराने हेतु आवेदन महोदय, सविनय निवेदन है कि मेरा नाम अनुभव राठौर S/O भूपेन्द्र सिंह राठौर है। मेरा खाता आपके बैंक में संचालित है, जिसका विवरण निम्नलिखित है— खाता संख्या: 0169000109747192 IFSC कोड: PUNB0016900 पिछले 9 दिनों से मेरे खाते में ऑनलाइन लेन-देन की सुविधा बंद है, जिसके कारण मुझे काफी परेशानी का सामना करना पड़ रहा है। महोदय, मैं एक छात्र हूँ और बाहर रहकर पढ़ाई कर रहा हूँ, इसलिए मेरे लिए ऑनलाइन ट्रांजैक्शन अत्यंत आवश्यक है। अतः आपसे विनम्र निवेदन है कि कृपया मेरे खाते की वार्षिक क्रेडिट सीमा (Annual Credit Limit) को संशोधित करते हुए मेरे खाते में ऑनलाइन लेन-देन की सुविधा जल्द से जल्द पुनः चालू करने की कृपा करें। इसके लिए मैं आपका सदा आभारी रहूँगा। धन्यवाद। भवदीय, नाम: अनुभव राठौर मोबाइल नंबर: 9219152898 पता: ग्राम बरौन, पोस्ट बरौन, फर्रुखाबाद दिनांक: 07 / 04 / 2026

Retrieval augmented generation combines a retriever with a language model. The retriever fetches relevant knowledge and the language model uses that knowledge to produce grounded answers.

Artificial intelligence is a branch of computer science that focuses on creating systems capable of tasks that normally require human intelligence. These tasks include reasoning, learning, language understanding, planning, and perception.
MongoDB is a document-oriented NoSQL database often used for session memory, chat history, and application state where flexible JSON-like documents are useful.

Assignment -1 Soft Computing Technique (NAI-602) 1. Explain the architecture of neural networks with suitable diagrams. 2. Differentiate between single layer feed-forward network and multilayer feed- forward network. 3. Explain recurrent neural networks and their applications. 4. Describe various learning techniques in neural networks. 5. Explain auto-associative memory and hetero-associative memory with examples. 6. Compare biological neurons and artificial neurons. 7. Explain the working of a perceptron with example of training for AND logic 8. Explain the architecture of Back Propagation Network with diagram. 9. Describe the Back Propagation learning algorithm step by step. 10. Explain the training process of multilayer perceptron networks. 11. Discuss the factors affecting back propagation training. 12. Explain how single layer neural networks are solved using perceptron learning. 13

f multilayer perceptron networks. 11. Discuss the factors affecting back propagation training. 12. Explain how single layer neural networks are solved using perceptron learning. 13. There is three-layered multi-layer perceptron in fig. apply backpropagation algorithm Note: Last date of Submission 14/03/2026

NAI-501 FUNDAMENTALS OF DATA ANALYTICS Assignment: 3 Unit – 3 Data Analysis 1. What is hypothesis testing? Explain why hypothesis testing is used in data analysis. Write the main steps of hypothesis testing in simple words. 2. What is a null hypothesis (H₀) and what is an alternative hypothesis (H₁)? Explain both and give three simple examples of H₀ and H₁ from daily life. 3. What do you mean by Type I error and Type II error in hypothesis testing? Explain both errors with easy, real-life examples. 4. What is level of significance (α) in hypothesis testing? Why is the 5% level of significance commonly used? Explain in simple language. 5. What is a p-value? How is p-value used to decide whether to reject or not reject the null hypothesis? Explain with a small, simple example. 6. What is a Z-test? When do we use the Z-test in hypothesis testing? Write the main conditions and give one simpl

reject the null hypothesis? Explain with a small, simple example. 6. What is a Z-test? When do we use the Z-test in hypothesis testing? Write the main conditions and give one simple example where Z-test can be applied. 7. What is a t-test? When is the t-test used instead of the Z-test? Explain with a simple example, such as comparing the average marks of students in a small class. 8. What is regression analysis? Explain the basic idea of regression as finding a relationship between one dependent variable and one independent variable, with a simple example (for example, study hours and exam marks).

DSA Practice – Reality Check (1 Round vs 2 Rounds) This document explains what actually happens if you solve the curated DSA question list one time vs two times, based on real interview expectations (no motivation, no hype). Total Questions: ~230–240 (high-quality, interview-focused) Case 1: One Complete Round  Pattern recognition: ~60%  Easy questions: Comfortable  Medium questions: Inconsistent  New questions: Need hints  Interview readiness: Basic / Borderline Overall DSA strength: ~55–60% Case 2: Two Complete Rounds  Pattern recognition: ~85–90%  Easy questions: Instant recall  Medium questions: Confident  New questions: Approach starts clicking  Interview readiness: Strong Overall DSA strength: ~80–85% Important Truth  One round builds understanding  Second round builds recall & ownership  DSA is pattern recognition, not memorization  3+ rounds give diminishing returns

80–85% Important Truth  One round builds understanding  Second round builds recall & ownership  DSA is pattern recognition, not memorization  3+ rounds give diminishing returns for most roles Final Verdict: Doing this list twice is sufficient for most product and service-based company interviews, provided the second round is done without immediately seeing solutions.

Data Mining and Warehousing Unit I – Introduction to Data Mining 1. Explain the concept of data mining and discuss its major functionalities used in knowledge discovery. 2. Describe the importance of data preprocessing in data mining and explain different data cleaning techniques used to handle missing or noisy data. 3. Discuss the evolution of database technology and explain why data mining became necessary. 4. Explain different forms of data preprocessing, including data integration and data transformation, with suitable examples. 5. Write a detailed note on data reduction techniques such as dimensionality reduction, numerosity reduction, and data compression. Unit II – Concept Description and Association Rules 1. Define data generalization and explain how analytical characterization is

and Association Rules 1. Define data generalization and explain how analytical characterization is used in describing data patterns. 2. Explain how statistical measures like mean, median, and standard deviation help in summarizing large databases. 3. Describe the process of mining association rules from large transactional databases. 4. Explain the Apriori algorithm used for discovering frequent itemsets with a suitable example. 5. A transactional database contains the following transactions: T1: Milk, Bread, Butter T2: Bread, Butter T3: Milk, Bread T4: Milk, Butter T5: Bread, Butter If the minimum support is 40% and minimum confidence is 60%, determine: (a) The frequent itemsets (b) The association rules generated from them. Unit III – Classification and Prediction 1. Explain the concept

sociation rules generated from them. Unit III – Classification and Prediction 1. Explain the concept of classification in data mining and discuss its applications. 2. Describe the Decision Tree method for classification with advantages and limitations. 3. Explain the Bayesian classification technique used in predictive analysis. 4. Discuss the k-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier and its working principle. 5. Explain the Backpropagation algorithm used in neural networks for classification and prediction. Unit IV – Cluster Analysis 1. Define cluster analysis and explain the different types of clustering methods used in data mining. 2. Describe the hierarchical clustering techniques, including CURE and Chameleon. 3. Explain the density-based clustering algorithms DBSCAN and OPTICS. 4. Discuss

CURE and Chameleon. 3. Explain the density-based clustering algorithms DBSCAN and OPTICS. 4. Discuss the grid-based clustering methods, including STING and CLIQUE. 5. Consider the dataset: 2, 4, 10, 12, 20, 22. Using K-means clustering with K = 2 and initial centroids C1 = 2 and C2 = 20: (a) Assign the data points to clusters. (b) Calculate the new centroids after one iteration. Unit V – Data Warehousing 1. Define a data warehouse and explain how it differs from a traditional database system. 2. Explain the multidimensional data model and the concept of data cubes. 3. Describe the star schema, snowflake schema, and fact constellation schema used in data warehousing. 4. Explain the three-tier architecture of a data warehouse with a suitable diagram. 5. Write a detailed note on the data ware
architecture of a data warehouse with a suitable diagram. 5. Write a detailed note on the data warehouse delivery process and data marts.

Environment The word "Environment" is derived from the French word "Environner," which means to encircle or surround • The Concept: It is defined as the sum total of all conditions and influences that affect the life and development of an organism. • Components: It includes all living (biotic) things, non-living (abiotic) things, and man- made properties. Segments of the Environment The environment is traditionally divided into four main spheres: • Atmosphere: The protective gaseous layer surrounding the Earth. • Hydrosphere: All water bodies, including oceans, rivers, and groundwater. • Lithosphere: The Earth's crust and soil layer. • Biosphere: The "life zone" where the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and lithosphere interact to support living organisms. Classification of Environment The lectur

here, and lithosphere interact to support living organisms. Classification of Environment The lecture categorizes the environment into two primary types: • Natural Environment: Systems that operate without human interference (e.g., oceans, forests, wild grasslands). • Man-Made (Anthropogenic) Environment: Environments created or heavily modified by humans to suit their needs, such as cities, air -conditioned rooms, or agricultural fields. Environmental Science Environmental Science is the scientific study of the physical components of the earth and how living organisms interact with them. Like assessing how factories and vehicles contribute to smoke/pollution, how deforestation reduces oxygen, and the scientific causes behind the depletion of the ozone layer and global warming. • Core Focu

n, and the scientific causes behind the depletion of the ozone layer and global warming. • Core Focus: Studying the Earth, air, and water. • Impact Analysis: Investigating how living organisms (especially humans) affect the environment. Ecology and Ecosystems While "Environment" is the surroundings, Ecology is the scientific study of the interactions between organisms and their environment. • Biotic Factors: Living components like plants, animals, and microorganisms. • Abiotic Factors: Non-living components like sunlight, temperature, water, and minerals. • Interaction: The lecture emphasizes that the quality of the environment directly impacts the development and behavior of the living beings within it. Ecosystem: “Nature’s web of life” A biological community of interacting organisms and

gs within it. Ecosystem: “Nature’s web of life” A biological community of interacting organisms and their natural surroundings is called an ecosystem. Living things (plants, animals, microbes) and non-living things (weather, sun, soil, climate) coexist in this intricate " community " to create a bubble of life. Every ecosystem is built on two main components: Biotic Factors (Living): Plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria. Abiotic Factors (Non -living): Sunlight, temperature, soil/sand/silt, water, and atmospheric gases. Types of Ecosystems Terrestrial (Land-based): Forests, Grasslands, Deserts, and Tundra. Aquatic (Water-based): (Freshwater): Rivers, lakes, and ponds. Marine: Oceans, coral reefs, and estuaries. The Energy Flow: • Producers: Green plants and algae that turn sunlight into ene

efs, and estuaries. The Energy Flow: • Producers: Green plants and algae that turn sunlight into energy via photosynthesis. • Consumers: Animals that eat plants (herbivores) or other animals (carnivores). • Decomposers: Fungi and bacteria that break down dead matter (turning it back into the soil/silt we discussed earlier) to be used again by plants. Energy Flow and the 10% Rule Energy enters an ecosystem primarily through solar radiation, which is harvested by plants (producers) and converted into biomass via photosynthesis. • Net Primary Productivity (NPP): This is the rate at which plants store energy as biomass. It is measured using various techniques like the harvest method, CO2 sensors, or even satellite imagery for large forest canopies. • The 10% Law: As energy moves up through tro

, or even satellite imagery for large forest canopies. • The 10% Law: As energy moves up through trophic levels (producers → primary consumers → secondary consumers → tertiary consumers), only about 10% of the energy is transferred to the next level. • Ecological Efficiency: This efficiency reflects how much biomass is stored at one level compared to the energy intake. Energy is "lost" to the environment through respiration, excretion, and undigested waste. 2. Levels of Ecological Organization Life is organized in a hierarchical structure that ecology specifically focuses on from the individual level upward. • Organism: A single individual (e.g., a blackbuck) • Population: A group of individuals of the same species living in a specific area. • Community: Different populations of various sp

uals of the same species living in a specific area. • Community: Different populations of various species coexisting in the same place. • Ecosystem: A community interacting with its nonliving environment (matter and energy). These often lack rigid boundaries and merge through ecotones (buffer zones). • Biosphere: The global sum of all ecosystems where life exists across air, water, and soil. 3. The Concept of the Ecological Niche A "niche" describes the specific role and unique position a species holds within an ecosystem. • Niche vs. Habitat: While a habitat is where a species lives, a niche is its "trade" or "job". • Competitive Exclusion Principle: Two species with nearly identical food habits cannot coexist in the same niche for long; one will eventually outcompete the other. • Functio

abits cannot coexist in the same niche for long; one will eventually outcompete the other. • Functional Niche: For example, a sunbird’s niche includes gathering nectar and aiding in plant pollination. Ecological Succession The slow and predictable process through which a biological community's structure changes over time is known as ecological succession. It is the process where a community is replaced by a "better competitor" species as environmental conditions change. Two primary categories of succession exist: The Seral Stages: Pioneer Species: The first organisms to inhabit a barren area. They are often "undemanding" and can survive harsh, nutrient-poor conditions. Transition Communities: The intermediate stages between the pioneer species and the climax stage. Each stage is called a "

: The intermediate stages between the pioneer species and the climax stage. Each stage is called a "sere". Climax Community: The final, stable stage of succession that remains relatively unchanged unless a major disturbance occurs. Types of Succession Based on Site Condition: Primary Succession: Starts in an area where no life previously existed (e.g., bare rock, new volcanic islands). It is a very slow process. Secondary Succession: Occurs in areas where a previous community was destroyed (e.g., by fire or flood) but the soil remains. This process is much faster Based on Environment: Hydrosere (Water), Lithosere (Rock), Psammosere (Sand), and Halosere (Saline soil). Based on Nutrition: Autotrophic Succession: Begins with green plants (producers). Dominated by green plants most of the succ

trophic Succession: Begins with green plants (producers). Dominated by green plants most of the succession are the example of autotropic succession, in autotropic succession initially inorganic environment is present. Heterotrophic Succession: Begins with bacteria and fungi feeding on dead organic matter; biodiversity often decreases over time in this model. Based on Cause: Autogenic: The community itself changes the environment (e.g., changing soil pH), making it unsuitable for itself but better for the next stage. Allogenic: External factors like human activity or climate change drive the replacement. Biodiversity is defined as the variety and variability among living organisms from all sources, including terrestrial, marine, and other aquatic ecosystems. It is fundamentally the "variety

urces, including terrestrial, marine, and other aquatic ecosystems. It is fundamentally the "variety of life" and is analyzed at three distinct levels: Biodiversity Levels • Genetic diversity: Variation of genes within a single species This enables species to adjust to changes in the climate or diseases. • Species diversity: The variety of species found in a given area is known as species diversity. Compared to a desert, a tropical rainforest has a higher species diversity. • Ecosystem diversity: refers to the range of ecological processes and habitats. Hotspots of biodiversity: Regions with high levels of species richness and significant threats to habitat, these hotspots covering 2.3% of the total world’s land area are found to have about 50% of the terrestrial biodiversity. Major threat

he total world’s land area are found to have about 50% of the terrestrial biodiversity. Major threats to biodiversity 1. Loss of Habitat This is the single greatest threat to biodiversity. As human populations expand, natural landscapes are converted for agriculture, urban development, and infrastructure. 2. Invasive Alien Species When a non-native species is introduced (intentionally or accidentally) into a new environment, it may lack natural predators 3. Population Growth and Over-exploitation The sheer number of humans drives the demand for resources. When we harvest a species faster than it can reproduce, it faces extinction. • Overfishing: Modern industrial fishing has depleted 90% of the world’s large predatory fish. • Poaching: The illegal trade of rhino horns, elephant ivory, and

the world’s large predatory fish. • Poaching: The illegal trade of rhino horns, elephant ivory, and tiger parts continues to push these "charismatic megafauna" toward the brink. 4. Pollution Pollution changes the chemical and physical makeup of habitats, making them uninhabitable for sensitive species. • Nutrient Pollution: Excess fertilizers cause Eutrophication, leading to "dead zones" in lakes and oceans where no oxygen exists for fish to breathe. • Biomagnification: Toxic chemicals (like mercury or DDT) increase in concentration as they move up the food chain, severely affecting top predators. 5. Climate Change Global warming is shifting the "climate envelopes" of species faster than many can migrate or adapt. • Ocean Acidification: Increased CO2 absorbed by oceans makes water more aci

y can migrate or adapt. • Ocean Acidification: Increased CO2 absorbed by oceans makes water more acidic, dissolving the calcium carbonate shells of corals and mollusks. • Loss of Seasonal Timing: Climate change disrupts the timing of migration and flowering (phenology). For example, birds may arrive at breeding grounds before their insect food source has emerged. Biodiversity conservation Strategies To combat these threats, two main conservation methods are discussed: • In-Situ Conservation: Protecting species within their natural habitat (e.g., Biosphere Reserves, National Parks, and Wildlife Sanctuaries). • Ex-Situ Conservation: Moving species out of their original habitat to nurture them elsewhere (e.g., Gene Banks, Seed Banks, Zoos, and Botanical Gardens).
